data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for their test project. In .NET, `dotnet test` supports hot reload via `dotnet test --hotreload` or by setting the `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable. But since they're using the new testing platform (`TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`), they can use `dotnet test --hotreload`.

Actually, for .NET test projects, the "hot reload" for tests is typically done via `dotnet watch test`. But the user mentioned they don't want to set an environment variable every time.

Let me think about this more carefully. With the Microsoft Testing Platform (which this project uses via `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`), hot reload can be enabled via:


- The `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable
- Or by passing `--hotreload` to the test runner

Since the user wants it always enabled without manually setting env vars each time, I should consider using `dotnet watch test` which automatically re-runs tests on file changes, or configuring it in the project file itself. A `.runsettings` file could also work, though the simplest approach might be setting the environment variable in a `Directory.Build.props` file so it persists across sessions.

For the Microsoft Testing Platform specifically, I'm trying to recall the exact MSBuild property name that controls hot reload behavior—it might be something like `<HotReloadEnabled>` or similar in the project file, but I'm not entirely certain of the exact syntax. I'm not entirely sure about the exact MSBuild property name for this, so let me consider the most practical approaches. Using `dotnet watch test` would enable hot reload automatically, but that changes the command itself. The cleaner option is probably setting the environment variable through a `.runsettings` file, which keeps the standard `dotnet test` command while still configuring hot reload at the project level.

Configuring the `.runsettings` file... Setting MSBuild properties directly might not work for this, so the most reliable approach is creating a `.runsettings` file with the environment variable and configuring the project to always use it, or alternatively setting `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART` as an environment variable that gets passed through to the test runner. Configuring the `.runsettings` file... Let me reconsider the environment variable approach—it needs to be set before the test platform starts, not just within the test host process. I think `testingplatformconfig.json` might actually be the cleanest solution here, though a `.runsettings` file with the environment variable should also work if configured properly. Let me go with the most reliable option. I'm second-guessing whether `.runsettings` environment variables actually reach the test platform infrastructure that needs them, since they're set in the test host process. But actually, thinking about this differently—when the user mentions "hot reload" for tests, they probably mean `dotnet watch test`, which automatically reruns tests when files change without needing any special environment variables at all.

The real question is whether they want `dotnet test` itself to behave like `dotnet watch test` automatically, which isn't really possible without changing the command they run. But if they're looking for the Microsoft Testing Platform's built-in hot reload feature that keeps the test process alive and watches for changes, I'd need to configure that through the project file or a testing platform configuration file. Let me go with setting this up through the project configuration directly. I'm second-guessing whether `.runsettings` actually processes environment variables before the test platform reads them, so let me think through the most straightforward approach—setting the environment variable directly in the project configuration through `.runsettings` is the standard .NET pattern, even if I'm not entirely certain about the timing.

Actually, rereading what the user wants—they're currently setting an environment variable manually each time and want to bake it into the project so hot reload is always enabled when running tests from the command line. The `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable is likely what they're referring to. Rather than relying on environment variables, I could configure this through the project file using MSBuild properties, or set it up in a `.runsettings` file that gets referenced in the csproj. Let me think about the simplest approach here.

Actually, `dotnet watch test` might be exactly what they need—it automatically watches for file changes and reruns tests without requiring any environment variable configuration. The fact that they're specifically mentioning an environment variable suggests they might be trying to enable hot reload in a different context, but `dotnet watch test` is the standard way to get automatic test reruns.

Let me think about setting the environment variable directly in the project file using an MSBuild target that runs before the test execution.

Setting environment variables in MSBuild...

Actually, I should reconsider the `.runsettings` approach since it's the standard, documented way to configure testing. But I'm realizing the Microsoft Testing Platform might have its own configuration system separate from the older VSTest framework, so I need to figure out what configuration method it actually uses. The environment variable approach works, but I'm wondering if there's a way to bake the `--hotreload` flag into the project configuration itself. Let me think through whether `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` is actually a valid property in the csproj, or if I need to go with a `.runsettings` file instead.

Actually, the `.runsettings` approach is cleaner—I can define the environment variable there and reference the file from the csproj, which keeps everything in one place and makes it the default for all test runs. Let me verify that `.runsettings` environment variables actually reach the Microsoft Testing Platform's test host process, since that's where `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` needs to be read.

Actually, there's a timing issue here—the test platform might initialize and decide whether to enable hot reload before the `.runsettings` environment variables are applied. A more reliable approach would be setting the environment variable at the MSBuild level through a `Directory.Build.targets` file, ensuring it's available before the test process even starts.

But thinking about this differently, I could configure the test runner directly through the project file or a `testingplatformconfig.json` file instead. The Microsoft Testing Platform supports configuration through JSON, environment variables, or command-line arguments, so I should explore what the JSON configuration structure looks like for enabling hot reload.

Generating JSON config... I'm going to add the hot reload configuration directly to the `.csproj` file by setting the appropriate property for the Microsoft Testing Platform, since that's the standard way to enable it in .NET 9 projects. I'm realizing that hot reload needs to be enabled at the infrastructure level rather than just as an environment variable, so `dotnet watch test` or the `--hotreload` flag is the actual solution here. I could try using MSBuild properties like `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` to make this the default, but I'm not entirely confident those exist. I think the most practical approach is to show the `.runsettings` configuration alongside mentioning `dotnet watch test` as the better alternative for hot reload specifically. I keep going in circles here. Let me just implement the `.runsettings` approach with the environment variable configured there, add the file path to the csproj, and test it. If that doesn't work, I'll explore other options, but I need to stop second-guessing myself and actually try something.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/testing/unit-testing-mstest-runner-intro:
There are several ways to run MSTest tests depending on your needs. You can run tests from an IDE (for example, Visual Studio, Visual Studio Code, or JetBrains Rider), or from the command line, or from a CI service (such as GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps).

Historically, MSTest relied on [VSTest](https://github.com/microsoft/vstest) for running tests in all contexts but starting with version 3.2.0, MSTest has its own test runner. This new runner is more lightweight and faster than VSTest, and it's the recommended way to run MSTest tests.

## VSTest vs MTP

MSTest supports running tests with both VSTest and [Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP)](microsoft-testing-platform-intro). The support for MTP is powered by the MSTest runner, which can run tests in all contexts (for example, continuous integration (CI) pipelines, CLI, Visual Studio Test Explorer, and VS Code Text Explorer). The MSTest runner is embedded directly in your MSTest test projects, and there are no other app dependencies, such as `vstest.console` or `dotnet test`, needed to run your tests. However, you can still run your tests using `dotnet test`.

The MSTest runner is open source and builds on the [MTP](microsoft-testing-platform-intro) library. You can find `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` code in the [microsoft/testfx](https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/tree/main/src/Platform/Microsoft.Testing.Platform) GitHub repository. The MSTest runner comes bundled with `MSTest in 3.2.0` or newer.

## Enable MTP in an MSTest project

It's recommended to use [MSTest SDK](unit-testing-mstest-sdk) as it greatly simplifies your project configuration and updating the project, and it ensures a proper alignment of the versions of the platform (MTP) and its extensions.

When you use `MSTest SDK`, by default you're opted in to using MTP.

```
<Project Sdk="MSTest.Sdk/4.1.0">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

Alternatively, you can enable MSTest runner by adding the `EnableMSTestRunner` property and setting `OutputType` to `Exe` in your project file. You also need to ensure that you're using `MSTest 3.2.0` or newer. We strongly recommend you update to the latest MSTest version available.

Consider the following example project file:

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Enable Microsoft.Testing.Platform, this is an opt-in feature -->
    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!--
      Displays error on console in addition to the log file. Note that this feature comes with a performance impact.
      For more information, visit https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/unit-testing-with-dotnet-test#show-failure-per-test
      -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>true</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>

    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!--
      MSTest meta package is the recommended way to reference MSTest.
      It's equivalent to referencing:
          Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk
          MSTest.TestAdapter
          MSTest.TestFramework
          MSTest.Analyzers
      Starting with 3.8, it also includes:
          Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.TrxReport
          Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.CodeCoverage
    -->
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="4.1.0" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Tip

To ensure all test projects in your solution use the MSTest runner, set the `EnableMSTestRunner` and `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` properties in _Directory.Build.props_ file instead of individual project files.

## Configurations and filters

### .runsettings

MTP supports the [runsettings](microsoft-testing-platform-extensions-vstest-bridge#runsettings-support) through the command-line option `--settings`. For the full list of supported MSTest entries, see [Configure MSTest: Runsettings](unit-testing-mstest-configure#runsettings). The following commands show various usage examples.

Using `dotnet run`:

```
dotnet run --project Contoso.MyTests -- --settings config.runsettings
```

Using `dotnet exec`:

```
dotnet exec Contoso.MyTests.dll --settings config.runsettings
```

\-or-

```
dotnet Contoso.MyTests.dll --settings config.runsettings
```

Using the executable:

```
Contoso.MyTests.exe --settings config.runsettings
```

### Tests filter

You can provide the tests [filter](selective-unit-tests?pivots=mstest#mstest-examples) seamlessly using the command line option `--filter`. The following commands show some examples.

Using `dotnet run`:

```
dotnet run --project Contoso.MyTests -- --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

Using `dotnet exec`:

```
dotnet exec Contoso.MyTests.dll --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

\-or-

```
dotnet Contoso.MyTests.dll --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

Using the executable:

```
Contoso.MyTests.exe --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

## See also

*   [Testing with `dotnet test`](unit-testing-with-dotnet-test)
*   [Filter tests](selective-unit-tests)
*   [Order unit tests](order-unit-tests)

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/testing/unit-testing-mstest-runner-intro:
There are several ways to run MSTest tests depending on your needs. You can run tests from an IDE (for example, Visual Studio, Visual Studio Code, or JetBrains Rider), or from the command line, or from a CI service (such as GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps).

Historically, MSTest relied on [VSTest](https://github.com/microsoft/vstest) for running tests in all contexts but starting with version 3.2.0, MSTest has its own test runner. This new runner is more lightweight and faster than VSTest, and it's the recommended way to run MSTest tests.

## VSTest vs MTP

MSTest supports running tests with both VSTest and [Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP)](microsoft-testing-platform-intro). The support for MTP is powered by the MSTest runner, which can run tests in all contexts (for example, continuous integration (CI) pipelines, CLI, Visual Studio Test Explorer, and VS Code Text Explorer). The MSTest runner is embedded directly in your MSTest test projects, and there are no other app dependencies, such as `vstest.console` or `dotnet test`, needed to run your tests. However, you can still run your tests using `dotnet test`.

The MSTest runner is open source and builds on the [MTP](microsoft-testing-platform-intro) library. You can find `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` code in the [microsoft/testfx](https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/tree/main/src/Platform/Microsoft.Testing.Platform) GitHub repository. The MSTest runner comes bundled with `MSTest in 3.2.0` or newer.

## Enable MTP in an MSTest project

It's recommended to use [MSTest SDK](unit-testing-mstest-sdk) as it greatly simplifies your project configuration and updating the project, and it ensures a proper alignment of the versions of the platform (MTP) and its extensions.

When you use `MSTest SDK`, by default you're opted in to using MTP.

```
<Project Sdk="MSTest.Sdk/4.1.0">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

Alternatively, you can enable MSTest runner by adding the `EnableMSTestRunner` property and setting `OutputType` to `Exe` in your project file. You also need to ensure that you're using `MSTest 3.2.0` or newer. We strongly recommend you update to the latest MSTest version available.

Consider the following example project file:

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Enable Microsoft.Testing.Platform, this is an opt-in feature -->
    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    <!--
      Displays error on console in addition to the log file. Note that this feature comes with a performance impact.
      For more information, visit https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/unit-testing-with-dotnet-test#show-failure-per-test
      -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>true</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>

    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!--
      MSTest meta package is the recommended way to reference MSTest.
      It's equivalent to referencing:
          Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk
          MSTest.TestAdapter
          MSTest.TestFramework
          MSTest.Analyzers
      Starting with 3.8, it also includes:
          Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.TrxReport
          Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.CodeCoverage
    -->
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="4.1.0" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Tip

To ensure all test projects in your solution use the MSTest runner, set the `EnableMSTestRunner` and `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` properties in _Directory.Build.props_ file instead of individual project files.

## Configurations and filters

### .runsettings

MTP supports the [runsettings](microsoft-testing-platform-extensions-vstest-bridge#runsettings-support) through the command-line option `--settings`. For the full list of supported MSTest entries, see [Configure MSTest: Runsettings](unit-testing-mstest-configure#runsettings). The following commands show various usage examples.

Using `dotnet run`:

```
dotnet run --project Contoso.MyTests -- --settings config.runsettings
```

Using `dotnet exec`:

```
dotnet exec Contoso.MyTests.dll --settings config.runsettings
```

\-or-

```
dotnet Contoso.MyTests.dll --settings config.runsettings
```

Using the executable:

```
Contoso.MyTests.exe --settings config.runsettings
```

### Tests filter

You can provide the tests [filter](selective-unit-tests?pivots=mstest#mstest-examples) seamlessly using the command line option `--filter`. The following commands show some examples.

Using `dotnet run`:

```
dotnet run --project Contoso.MyTests -- --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

Using `dotnet exec`:

```
dotnet exec Contoso.MyTests.dll --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

\-or-

```
dotnet Contoso.MyTests.dll --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

Using the executable:

```
Contoso.MyTests.exe --filter "FullyQualifiedName~UnitTest1|TestCategory=CategoryA"
```

## See also

*   [Testing with `dotnet test`](unit-testing-with-dotnet-test)
*   [Filter tests](selective-unit-tests)
*   [Order unit tests](order-unit-tests)

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,
<exited with exit code 0>

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.

                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process.

data.reasoningText:
I see that `dotnet test` has a `-e` option to set environment variables. But the user doesn't want to do that every time. Let me look at the Microsoft Testing Platform documentation for hot reload specifically.

I also notice the project doesn't have `EnableMSTestRunner` or `OutputType` set to `Exe`, but it does have `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` set to `true`. This means it uses MTP through `dotnet test`.

Let me search for the hot reload documentation for Microsoft Testing Platform.
